Graphical abstract Abstract High strength aluminium alloys are used in thin-walled applications in the aerospace,  automotive  and  lightweight  structure  manufacturing  industries  because  of  their  important strength to weight ratio. Lower structural rigidity of thin wall part produces  chatter  vibration  during  thin  wall  milling,  which  leads  to  poor  surface  integrity,  dimensional  error,  and  endangering  milling  process.  Proposed  research  aims  to  perform an experimental analysis and understand the chatter nature and material  removal characteristics in thin-walled milling of AA7075-T6 aluminium alloy based on  Taguchi L27 orthogonal array design. Material removal rate (MRR), kurtosis index and  chatter frequency were tested with spindle speed, feed rate and radial depth of cut on  the systematic effects and were analysed from time domain and frequency domain.  Vibration signals obtained in manufacturing operations were processed to grasp the  value of kurtosis as an indicator of higher order of statistical chatter, and the chatter  frequency response was processed to find the dynamic instability trend with different  machini

items.nearby_content: Abstract High strength aluminium alloys are used in thin-walled applications in the aerospace,  automotive  and  lightweight  structure  manufacturing  industries  because  of  their  important strength to weight ratio. Lower structural rigidity of thin wall part produces  chatter  vibration  during  thin  wall  milling,  which  leads  to  poor  surface  integrity,  dimensional  error,  and  endangering  milling  process.  Proposed  research  aims  to  perform an experimental analysis and understand the chatter nature and material  removal characteristics in thin-walled milling of AA7075-T6 aluminium alloy based on  Taguchi L27 orthogonal array design. Material removal rate (MRR), kurtosis index and  chatter frequency were tested with spindle speed, feed rate and radial depth of cut on  the systematic effects and were analysed from time domain and frequency domain.  Vibration signals obtained in manufacturing operations were processed to grasp the  value of kurtosis as an indicator of higher order of statistical chatter, and the chatter  frequency response was processed to find the dynamic instability trend with different  machining conditions. items.label: Methodology

items.notes: Methodology confirmed by both DOC and PDF detection.
items.nearby_content:
2.1 Material and Tool Specifications
The  experimental  research  studies  were  conducted
using AA7075-T6 aluminium alloy thin plates. Workpieces  were made up of aluminium sheets of 40 x 80 x 3 mm  thickness, in a cantilever-like configuration used to machine  the  thin-wall.  Chemical  composition  of  the  material  was  determined with the help of spectrometric analysis, which  confirmed  that  the  major  alloying  elements  include  Zn  (~5.58%), Mg (~2.53%), Cu (~1.68%), and Cr (~0.22%),  which  provide  the  necessary  mechanical  strength  and  machining  characteristics.  The  milling  experiments  were  performed on a Vertical Machining Center (AMS MCV 300)  with  a  high-precision  spindle  and  guideways  with  linear  motion allowing the stable machining conditions and the  accurate vibration analysis.
Table 1. Machine, Tool, and Material Specifications
Workpiece Material AA7075-T6

items.label: Conclusion
items.evidence_text: Matched heading: "7. Conclusions" | Detection method: source_docx_text
items.notes: Conclusion confirmed by both DOC and PDF detection.
items.nearby_content: The present study covers the experimental investigation of  the chatter characteristics and material removal behaviour  in  thin-walled  milling  of  aluminium  alloy  AA7075  using  Taguchi orthogonal array L27 design. Time- and frequency- domain vibration characterization was used to investigate  material removal rate (MRR), the kurtosis index and chatter  frequency for different spindle speeds, feed rates and radial  depth of cut. Based on the Taguchi optimization results,  analysis of the S/N ratio and ANOVA results, this showed  that the radial depth of cut is the most influence parameter  on  MRR  followed  by  feed  rate  and  spindle  speed  parameter.  The  optimum  machining  parameters  for  the  maximum  MRR  were  determined  at  1500  rpm,  1.5  mm  radial  depth  of  cut  and  150  mm/min  feed  rate  and  regression coefficient was found to be very close to the  experimental responses showing good agreement between  the  predicted  and  experimental  responses  whereas  the  confirmation  experiment  showed  that  there  is  very  little  prediction  error  made  with  the  optimization  model  so  developed making it adequate and reliable.
